## Biggest GDPR Fines in 2023

### 1. Meta — €1.2 billion ($1.3 billion)

![meta-logo](https://termly.io/wp-content/uploads/meta-logo-1.png "meta-logo")

![meta-logo](https://termly.io/wp-content/uploads/meta-logo-1.png "meta-logo")

On May 22, 2023, the supervisory authority in Ireland slapped social media conglomerate Meta with [a record-breaking €1.2 billion fine](https://edpb.europa.eu/our-work-tools/consistency-findings/register-decisions/2023/decision-data-protection-commission_en).

#### Why They Were Fined

According to data privacy regulators, Meta was fined for transferring data collected from Facebook users in the EU/EEA to the US, violating GDPR international transfer guidelines.

The company failed to comply with the EU’s highest court’s Schrems II decision from 2020, invalidating the EU-S Privacy Shield Framework.

In addition to the massive fine, Meta has been told to stop the international transfers and has five months to comply with the corrections.

#### How They Responded

Meta announced that it plans to appeal the decision, likely leading to a lengthy legal process.

For now, they say Facebook services shouldn’t be interrupted but information like photos, friend connections, advertising data, and direct messages could eventually be impacted.

Check back for future updates.

### 2. Meta Platforms Ireland Limited — €390 million ($426 million)

![meta-logo](https://termly.io/wp-content/uploads/meta-logo-1.png "meta-logo")

![meta-logo](https://termly.io/wp-content/uploads/meta-logo-1.png "meta-logo")

On January 2, 2023, Ireland’s supervisory authority hit Meta Platform Ireland Limited, the data controller in the region, [with a €390 million fine](https://dataprotection.ie/en/news-media/data-protection-commission-announces-conclusion-two-inquiries-meta-ireland).

#### Why They Were Fined

Meta had asked users to consent to new terms and conditions that changed the legal basis for their data processing from consent to fulfillment of a contract.

Refusing consent blocked users from accessing their accounts.

The DPC found Meta in violation of the transparency guidelines described by the GDPR.

Additionally, they found that the company didn’t give enough clarity on why the processing of personal data is necessary and on what legal basis, as outlined in their terms and conditions.

Some EU regulators also claimed it was unreasonable to cite fulfillment of a contract as a legal basis for using Meta’s services, and the matter was sent to the EDPB.

The EDPB upheld this criticism and fined Meta for €390 million.

#### How They Responded

Meta intends to appeal the fine and the decision made by regulatory authorities.

In a statement, Meta references that there’s been a lack of agreement regarding the practical application of the legal basis for sharing personal information.

### 3. TikTok Limited — €345 million ($378 million)

![TikTok-Logo](https://termly.io/wp-content/uploads/TikTok-Logo.webp "TikTok-Logo")

![TikTok-Logo](https://termly.io/wp-content/uploads/TikTok-Logo.webp "TikTok-Logo")

On September 1, 2023, the popular video-sharing service and social media platform TikTok received a €345 million fine for violating the GDPR from Ireland’s data protection authority.

#### Why They Were Fined

Ireland’s supervisory authority [fined TikTok Limited](https://edpb.europa.eu/system/files/2023-09/final_decision_tiktok_in-21-9-1_-_redacted_8_september_2023.pdf) for violating the general data processing principles outlined by the GDPR.

The DPC performed the investigation to determine how TikTok was handling the personal information of children under 13.

In particular, they cited that TikTok automatically set profiles of children to public.

#### How They Responded

Initially, some objections to the fine occurred.

Berlin’s supervisory authority called for violations under the principle of fairness because of TikTok’s alleged use of dark patterns.

On the other side of the spectrum, the Italian data protection authority claimed TikTok actually complied with the age-verification requirements.

The matter was referred to the European Data Protection Board (EDPB), who, after investigating, decided to add the additional violation to the fairness principle.

### 4. CRITEO — €40 million ($44 million)

![CRITEO-Logo](https://termly.io/wp-content/uploads/CRITEO-Logo-300x66.webp "CRITEO-Logo")

![CRITEO-Logo](https://termly.io/wp-content/uploads/CRITEO-Logo-300x66.webp "CRITEO-Logo")

On June 15, 2023, an online advertising company called CRITEO was fined by France’s data protection authority, CNIL, for €40 million.

#### Why They Were Fined

The [CNIL fined CRITEO](https://www.cnil.fr/fr/publicite-personnalisee-criteo-sanctionne-dune-amende-de-40-millions-deuros) after determining that the advertising company failed to ensure data subjects provided opt-in consent for the processing of their data.

The company was also fined for failing to inform data subjects about their rights and not providing a way to properly act on those rights.

#### How They Responded

CRITEO criticized the fine from the CNIL and denied the accusation that they failed to inform data subjects about their processing activities.

They also argued that the initial fine of €60 million was excessive compared to penalties issued by the CNIL to other companies for similar violations.

As a result, the fine was lowered to €40 million.

### 5. TikTok — €14.5 million ($15.8 million)

![TikTok-Logo](https://termly.io/wp-content/uploads/TikTok-Logo.webp "TikTok-Logo")

![TikTok-Logo](https://termly.io/wp-content/uploads/TikTok-Logo.webp "TikTok-Logo")

On April 4, 2023, the social media platform and video-sharing service TikTok received a €14.5 million fine from the UK’s data protection authority, the Information Commissioners Office (ICO), for violating the UK GDPR.

#### Why They Were Fined

The [ICO fined TikTok for collecting personal data that belonged to children](https://ico.org.uk/about-the-ico/media-centre/news-and-blogs/2023/04/ico-fines-tiktok-127-million-for-misusing-children-s-data/) without obtaining proper parental consent.

According to the investigation, in 2020, TikTok collected data from more than one million UK children younger than, contradicting their terms and conditions agreement.

It was determined that the company did not do enough to verify who was using their platform and sufficiently remove underage children who made accounts.

#### How They Responded

Initially, the ICO imposed a higher fine of €31.3 million but lowered it after deciding not to pursue an additional breach related to TikTok allegedly using special categories of data.

The decision cut the fine in half, and TikTok has invested in updating their security measures and internal processing systems.

### 6. Axpo Italia S.p.A. — €10 million ($10.9 million)

![Axpo-Italia-SpA-logo](https://termly.io/wp-content/uploads/Axpo-Italia-SpA-logo-300x119.webp "Axpo-Italia-SpA-logo")

![Axpo-Italia-SpA-logo](https://termly.io/wp-content/uploads/Axpo-Italia-SpA-logo-300x119.webp "Axpo-Italia-SpA-logo")

On September 9, 2023, Italy’s data protection authority, the Garante Per La Protezione Dei Dati Personali, fined the sustainable energy solutions company Axpo Italia Spa €10 million for violating parts of the GDPR.

#### Why They Were Fined

The [Garante investigated after receiving multiple complaints](https://www.garanteprivacy.it/web/guest/home/docweb/-/docweb-display/docweb/9485681) that Axpo Italia S.p.A. was processing inaccurate customer data to establish unsolicited contracts.

Specifically, the company’s salespeople were acquiring new electricity and gas contracts using a database of outdated information. No process or safeguard was in place to verify that the data was accurate and linked to real customers.

The Garante determined that more than 5,000 data subjects were impacted and found the company in violation of Articles 5(1),(a) and (d), Article 5(2), and Article 24 of the GDPR.

#### How They Responded

Axpo Italia S.p.A. complied with the findings and was ordered to implement corrective measures, including using a blocking system to verify the accuracy of their sales teams’ data and an alert system to detect fraudulent behaviors.

They were also ordered to halt any further processing activities of their customers who terminated their contracts due to unsolicited service activations.

### 7. TIM S.p.A. — €7.6 million ($8.3 million)

![TIM-logo](https://termly.io/wp-content/uploads/TIM-logo.webp "TIM-logo")

![TIM-logo](https://termly.io/wp-content/uploads/TIM-logo.webp "TIM-logo")

The Italian supervisory authority, Garante, found the telecommunications company [TIM S.p.A. to violate aspects of the GDPR](https://www.gpdp.it/web/guest/home/docweb/-/docweb-display/docweb/9895080).

#### Why they were fined

TIM S.p.A. was fined for inadequately responding to data subject requests and supervising illegal call centers.

The company also allegedly released personal data to public telephone directories without obtaining proper consent from the affected data subjects.

#### How they responded

TIM S.p.A. is expected to pay the fine, as this is not the first time they’ve received penalties for violating the GDPR.

### 8. WhatsApp Ireland Ltd. — €5.5 million ($6 million)

![WhatsApp-logo](https://termly.io/wp-content/uploads/WhatsApp-logo.png "WhatsApp-logo")

![WhatsApp-logo](https://termly.io/wp-content/uploads/WhatsApp-logo.png "WhatsApp-logo")

On January 19, 2023, [WhatsA](https://dataprotection.ie/en/news-media/data-protection-commission-announces-conclusion-inquiry-whatsapp)[pp Ireland Ltd. was fined by the Ireland data protection authority](https://dataprotection.ie/en/news-media/data-protection-commission-announces-conclusion-inquiry-whatsapp).

#### Why they were fined

WhatsApp received a fine for not complying with the GDPR definition of consent.

Users were asked to accept new terms and conditions by clicking a button that read “agree and continue,” which WhatsApp viewed as entering into a contract and used this as their legal basis for collecting personal data.

However, data subjects complained that the company was relying on consent and instead of it being freely given, WhatsApp was forcing individuals into agreeing to the new terms.

The DPC did not uphold the argument that WhatsApp was actually seeking consent but found the company in violation of transparency in relation to their legal basis for processing.

#### How they responded

WhatsApp responded by changing its legal basis for processing most user data to legitimate interest, and it says this does not change its commitment to user privacy or the way it treats user data.

### 9. EOS Matrix d.o.o. — €5.4 million ($5.8 million)

![EOS Matrix Logo](https://termly.io/wp-content/uploads/EOS-Matrix-Logo.webp "EOS Matrix Logo")

![EOS Matrix Logo](https://termly.io/wp-content/uploads/EOS-Matrix-Logo.webp "EOS Matrix Logo")

On October 5, 2023, after receiving a petition containing a USB stick with information about 181,641 individuals, the [Croatian Data Protection Agency (AZOP) fined a debt collection company called EOS Matrix d.o.o.](https://azop.hr/debt-collection-agency-eos-matrix-d-o-o-imposed-with-administrative-fine-in-the-amount-of-5-47-million-euros/) for violating portions of the GDPR.

#### Why they were fined

EOS Matrix violated the GDPR by not taking appropriate technical measures to protect the personal data of data subjects.

The group also processed personal data of subjects who were not engaged in a debtor/creditor relationship without establishing a legal basis. The processing involved special categories of data, which was not transparently communicated to data subjects.

#### How they responded

EOS Matrix d.o.o. denied aspects of the allegations, claiming that they do securely store their consumers’ personal data.

However, AZOP did not uphold their denial, as it is still unclear how the personal data of their 181,641 customers was exfiltrated and copied to the USB drive in the first place.

### 10. Clearview AI — €5.2 million ($5.6 million)

![Clearview-AI-Logo](https://termly.io/wp-content/uploads/Clearview-AI-Logo.png "Clearview-AI-Logo")

![Clearview-AI-Logo](https://termly.io/wp-content/uploads/Clearview-AI-Logo.png "Clearview-AI-Logo")

On May 10, 2023, Clearview AI, the group responsible for ChatGPT, was fined by France’s CNIL.

#### Why they were fined

France’s [CNIL fined Clearview AI](https://www.legifrance.gouv.fr/cnil/id/CNILTEXT000047527412?init=true&page=1&query=SAN-2023-005&searchField=ALL&tab_selection=all) for insufficient cooperation with the supervisory authority.

Specifically, CNIL claimed that the company failed to comply with previous orders from December 2021, when the company was found processing millions of data without providing access rights to data subjects.

The company was ordered to stop collecting and processing the personal data of people in France but did not comply, leading to a combination of €100,000 of overdue fines per day over 52 days.

#### How they responded

Clearview AI denied all allegations, claiming it does not have customers in France or the EU.

## Biggest GDPR Fines in 2022

There were been multiple massive fines issued to companies violating the GDPR. This is because these organizations have had multiple years to comply with the EU’s data protection law and did not do so. As a result, they’re facing dramatic fines for violating EU citizens’ rights to data privacy.

### 1. Instagram — €405 million ($401.3 million)

![Instagram_logo](https://termly.io/wp-content/uploads/Instagram_logo.png "Instagram_logo")

![Instagram_logo](https://termly.io/wp-content/uploads/Instagram_logo.png "Instagram_logo")

Instagram, owned by Meta Platforms, was [fined by the Irish Data Protection Commissioner](https://edpb.europa.eu/news/news/2022/record-fine-instagram-following-edpb-intervention_en) in what had been the second the highest GDPR fine ever imposed. 

#### Why They Were Fined

Instagram was fined €405 million for violating rules on the processing of children’s data without a legal basis. Children between 13 and 17 years old had their phone numbers and email addresses publicly available if they operated a business or creator Instagram account.

#### How They Responded

Instagram stated that it [disagreed with the way the fine was calculated](https://www.euronews.com/next/2022/09/06/ireland-fines-instagram-405-million-over-protection-of-childrens-data) and was reviewing the decision.

### 2. Enel Energia — €26.5 Million ($29.27 Million)

![enel-energia-logo](https://termly.io/wp-content/uploads/enel-energia-logo.png "enel-energia-logo")

![enel-energia-logo](https://termly.io/wp-content/uploads/enel-energia-logo.png "enel-energia-logo")

Enel Energia, an international electricity and gas distributor headquartered in Italy, has received the **highest GDPR fine of 2022** so far.

#### Why They Were Fined

Italy’s data protection agency, called the Garante, chose to fine Enel Energia [€26.5 million](https://edpb.europa.eu/news/national-news/2022/aggressive-telemarketing-italian-sa-fines-enel-energia-eur-265-million_en) after receiving hundreds of complaints against the company.

The Garante’s investigation found that Enel Energia was using the personal data of its customers unlawfully. The company used this private data to perform telemarketing calls without getting appropriate user consent or informing users how their information would be used.

#### How They Responded

Enel Energia claims that the calls were performed to contact customers during the pandemic. In a statement emailed to [Compliance Week](https://www.complianceweek.com/regulatory-enforcement/italian-dpa-fines-enel-energia-301m-under-gdpr-over-telemarketing-practices/31274.article), Enel Energia stated i
